name: 1-2-3 Drill
goal: Train a proper load → stride → swing sequence with deliberate timing so every phase fires in the correct order.
description: Start with the bat on your shoulder and focus on a rhythmic three-step load process. On "one," shift your weight slightly back; on "two," lift your lead leg into a stride; and on "three," plant and swing smoothly. This drill is designed to help hitters find a repeatable tempo and avoid a static or rushed approach at the plate.

name: 1-2-3 Rhythm Tee
goal: Develop rhythm and timing in the swing with cleaner sequencing from load through launch.
description: Set up a ball on the tee and use a rhythmic "rock back, rock forward, and swing" motion to create momentum. The goal is to synchronize the hand load with the stride to ensure the body is moving in unison before making contact. It emphasizes fluid movement over a rigid, mechanical swing.
equipment: T. Baseball's net or screen to hit into.

name: 1st Base Flip To Pitcher
goal: To ensure the first baseman and pitcher execute smooth, accurate transfers for outs at first base
description: The first baseman fields a ground ball moving away from the base as the pitcher sprints over to cover. In one fluid motion, the first baseman underhands the ball softly to the pitcher's chest, leading them to the bag. This drill focuses on the timing, accuracy, and communication required to execute this common defensive play efficiently.
equipment: Gloves, bucket of baseballs, base, fungo bat

name: 1st Base Inside Receiving
goal: To train the first baseman to consistently handle inside throws by using proper footwork around the bag, maintaining contact with the base, and securing the catch without obstructing the advancing run
description: The first baseman sets up on the inside of first base, creating a direct throwing lane for the infielder. As the throw approaches, the player should step towards the ball with their glove-side foot, keeping their body open to the field while keeping their other foot on the bag. The main focus is on cleanly receiving the ball on the inside part of the base to secure the out while successfully avoiding the runner.
equipment: Gloves, bucket of baseballs, base, fungo bat

name: 1st Base Off Bag
goal: To improve a first baseman's range in fielding ground balls and to develop the footwork required to transition from a fielding position back to covering the bag.
description: The first baseman begins in a ready stance, positioned several steps off the bag toward second base. On a ground ball hit by a coach, the player works on fielding the ball cleanly with proper technique, focusing on footwork and glove work. After securing the ball, the player simulates a throw to another base or returns to first base, reinforcing the transition from fielder to covering the bag.

name: 1st Base Receiving
goal: To develop proper footwork, glove presentation, and receiving technique for first basemen to secure outs on throws from the infield.
description: Set up with your heels on the front of the bag, providing a large, clear target for the infielder. As the throw comes, step towards the ball with your glove-side foot, aiming to catch it out in front of your body. Emphasize quick, efficient footwork to adjust to off-target throws and secure the catch while maintaining contact with the base.

name: 1st Base Receiving Short Hops
goal: To develop a first baseman's skill and confidence in cleanly fielding short hops and handling difficult throws.
description: A coach or partner throws one-hop throws to the first baseman, who is positioned at the bag. The player should focus on getting their body in front of the ball and keeping their glove low to the ground. Emphasis is placed on reading the hop, using a soft glove to absorb the ball, and keeping the ball in front of them to make the out.

name: 2nd Baseman Backhand Flip
goal: To improve a second baseman's body control and soft hands when fielding a backhand ground ball and making a quick flip to start a double play.
description: From the ready position, the second baseman moves to their glove side to field a ground ball on their backhand. Without taking the ball out of the glove, the player uses momentum and a soft wrist action to execute a gentle underhand flip to the player covering second base. The focus is on maintaining body control and delivering an accurate, easy-to-handle toss while moving towards the target.

name: 2nd Baseman Drop Step Throw
goal: To train the second baseman to create space and proper throwing mechanics on balls hit to their glove-side.
description: From the second base position, the player fields a ground ball to their glove side, away from the bag. The player will then execute a drop step with their throwing-side foot, opening their hips to create alignment and momentum toward the target. After the transfer, the player makes a strong, accurate throw, focusing on a quick release and proper follow-through.

name: 2nd Baseman Forehand Spin
goal: To develop advanced body control and footwork, enabling the infielder to make a difficult play and throw accurately after a full rotation.
description: The drill begins with a coach rolling a ground ball to the 2nd baseman's forehand side, forcing them to move away from the base. Upon fielding the ball, the player executes a reverse spin, turning their body completely around while securing the grip. This movement allows them to quickly realign their shoulders and feet to make a strong and accurate throw to the target base.

name: 2nd Baseman Underhand Flip
goal: To develop a quick, efficient, and accurate underhand flip technique for close-range feeds, particularly during double plays.
description: From the second base position, field a ground ball and establish a firm grip. As you approach the bag, create momentum with your lower half by stepping toward your target. Use a firm wrist and a smooth, pendulum-like arm motion to deliver an accurate underhand flip to the chest area of the player covering the base.

name: 3 Plate Adjustment Drill
goal: Develop a hitter's timing and adjustability to different pitch speeds without changing their core swing mechanics.
description: Set up three home plates in a line toward the pitcher, spaced approximately 3–5 feet apart (typically at 10, 15, and 20 feet from the tosser).,A coach stands behind an L-screen and delivers front-toss pitches at a constant speed while the batter takes 3–5 swings at each plate, starting from the back and progressing forward (or vice versa).,Because the toss speed never changes, the distance determines the difficulty: the back plate simulates a slow off-speed pitch requiring the hitter to stay back and wait, while the front plate simulates a high-velocity fastball that forces the hitter to load earlier and use quicker hands.,The goal is for the hitter to maintain their core swing mechanics while adjusting their timing and rhythm so the front foot is down before contact at every station.

name: 30 Second Backhand
goal: To build a player's confidence and consistency in making the routine backhand play.
description: A coach or partner rolls a continuous stream of ground balls to the player's backhand side for 30 seconds. The player must maintain a low, athletic stance, moving their feet to create a good fielding angle for each ball. The emphasis is on watching the ball into the glove and using a clean, one-handed scoop.

name: 30 Second Backhand Cross
goal: To enhance a player's lateral agility, improve their backhand fielding mechanics, and develop muscle memory for quick, efficient transfers on difficult plays.
description: From an athletic fielding position, the player will react to a series of balls rolled or tossed to their backhand side for a continuous 30-second interval. The player must execute a quick crossover step, get their body in front of the ball, and cleanly field it with a backhand technique. After securing the ball, the player should simulate a strong and accurate throw to first base.

name: 30 Second Crow Hops
goal: To develop proper footwork and momentum for long throws, reinforcing the mechanics of a quick and efficient crow hop.
description: From a ready athletic position, an infielder will receive a ground ball or a feed from a coach and immediately execute a crow hop to simulate a long throw across the diamond. The player should focus on gaining ground toward their target with a quick, explosive hop, ensuring their feet are aligned upon landing to facilitate a powerful and accurate throw. This drill emphasizes rapid footwork and maintaining momentum through the throwing motion in a continuous, timed sequence.

name: 30 Second Grounders
goal: To develop the fielder's endurance, focus, and ability to maintain proper defensive mechanics while handling a high volume of repetitions in a short period.
description: A coach or partner hits a continuous series of ground balls to a fielder for 30 seconds without stopping. The fielder must maintain a low, athletic fielding posture, focusing on watching the ball into the glove and using quick feet to get in front of each grounder. After fielding, the player should practice a quick, clean exchange to the throwing hand before discarding the ball and preparing for the next repetition.

name: 30 Second Quick Toss
goal: To develop rapid glove-to-hand transfers, improve throwing accuracy under pressure, and enhance overall hand speed.
description: Two players stand approximately 10-15 feet apart in an athletic stance. Using one ball, they engage in a rapid-fire game of catch for 30 seconds, focusing on a quick, clean exchange from glove to throwing hand. The goal is to make as many accurate throws as possible to the partner's chest within the time limit, maintaining proper footwork and a consistent release point.

name: 30 Second Short Hops
goal: To improve an infielder's ability to handle difficult short hops by developing quick, soft hands and confidence.
description: A coach or partner kneels 10-15 feet away and throws a continuous series of short hops to a player in a ready fielding position for 30 seconds. The player should focus on keeping their glove out front, reading the bounce quickly, and using soft hands to absorb the ball. This drill emphasizes quick reactions and proper fielding mechanics under pressure.

name: 4 Corners
goal: To develop quick feet, soft hands, and rapid ball transfer for infielders.
description: Set up four cones in a diamond shape, with a player at each cone. The drill begins with a player at one cone throwing a groundball to the player at the next cone in a clockwise direction. Each player fields the ball and makes a quick, accurate throw to the next player in the sequence, continuing the pattern around the diamond.

name: 4 Corners Bunting
goal: To improve bat control and the ability to execute situational bunts successfully into specific target areas under pressure.
description: Place four targets or cones in the corners of the bunting zone and attempt to deaden the ball into each specific area. Focus on "catching" the ball with the barrel and using your legs to adjust to different heights rather than just your arms. This improves bat control and the ability to execute situational bunts under pressure.

name: 4.3 Drill
goal: Improve infield drill performance so that each position can consistently field the ball and make an accurate throw to first base in 4.3 seconds or less per attempt. This will enhance defensive speed,

name: 7 Ball Front Toss
goal: To improve hitting endurance and maintain mechanical efficiency under conditions of rapid repetition and fatigue.
description: A coach tosses seven balls in rapid succession to different areas of the strike zone, requiring the hitter to reset quickly between swings. This drill builds conditioning, improves hand-eye coordination, and forces the hitter to maintain short, compact paths to the ball as fatigue sets in.

name: 90/45/Even Progression Tee
goal: To master the ability to control ball direction by adjusting the contact point based on pitch location.
description: Position the tee at three different angles relative to the hitter's stance to practice directional hitting. Swing at balls set up for pull-side (90), up the middle (even), and opposite field (45) to learn how the contact point changes for each. This teaches the hitter how to let the ball travel or meet it out front based on location.

name: 90/45/Even Progression Tee
goal: Build better shoulder control and loading pattern by progressing from exaggerated positions into a normal stance.

name: Airplane Drill
goal: To teach hitters how to effectively integrate hip hinge and shoulder plane mechanics into their swing using the airplane drill.

name: Angle Flips
goal: Train hitters to stay inside the baseball and drive the ball back up the middle.
description: The coach sits at a 45-degree angle to the hitter and flips balls toward the plate, forcing the hitter to stay "inside" the ball to drive it back toward the coach. This prevents "pulling off" the ball and encourages a path that stays through the hitting zone for as long as possible.

name: Angled Force Plate Tee - Back Foot
goal: Goal: Hit the ball back up the middle, keeping the body weight inside the back foot
description:
The tee is set up in front of the middle of the plate.
,The hitter sets up even with the plate, while the partner places a ball on the tee.
,Place an angled force plate under the back foot.
,The objective is to hit the ball back up the middle using a standard loading pattern and stride, while keeping the hitter’s body weight on the back foot.,The angle of the force plate will prevent hitters from drifting backwards and losing their center of gravity
equipment: Tee, baseballs, net or screen to hit into, angled force plate

name: Angled Force Plate Tee Back Foot
goal: To improve ground force transmission and prevent back leg collapse by strengthening the push-off mechanics from the inside of the back foot.
description: Place a force plate or an angled block under the back foot to emphasize pushing off the inside of the foot rather than collapsing the ankle. This creates a stronger foundation and helps the hitter transfer energy from the ground up through the core. It is excellent for hitters who struggle with "spinning out" on their back side.

name: Anti Rotation Resistance Drill
goal: This drill helps hitters who struggle with over-rotating their shoulders or hips too early, which often leads to loss of power and poor swing mechanics. It benefits players working on posture, hip hin

name: Arm Path Drill
goal: To develop a consistent and efficient arm action that promotes throwing accuracy and reduces the risk of injury.
description: Begin in a balanced pitching stance with the ball in your glove at your chest. Break your hands and simulate your throwing motion in slow motion, focusing on a consistent and efficient arm path. Concentrate on achieving a proper 'scap load' position before moving through a fluid, circular motion to a high release point, building muscle memory for a smooth delivery.

name: Arm Speed
goal: To enhance the pitcher's natural arm speed and develop a more efficient, quick arm path to the release point.
description: From a standing or kneeling position, the pitcher performs rapid, controlled arm circles both forward and backward, focusing on a loose and fluid motion. The emphasis is on moving the arm as quickly as possible without tension, using a towel or a light training ball to ensure the focus remains on speed rather than force. This drill isolates the arm action to build neuromuscular speed and endurance.

name: Back Foot Cone Tee
goal: To reinforce proper balance and prevent the collapse of the back side during the swing.
description: Place a small plastic cone just behind the hitter's back foot to serve as a physical boundary. If the hitter's heel or weight collapses backward during the swing, they will tip the cone, providing immediate feedback on balance. This encourages a vertical axis and proper hip rotation without losing ground.

name: Back Hip Load Tee
goal: Teach hitters to load into the back hip with more control before launching forward.
description: Set up two tees — one in the middle of the plate (slightly off the front leg) and one behind the hitter near the back knee, angled with a baseball underneath. A coach or partner observes balance, direction, and load mechanics

name: Back Net Constraint Tee
goal: To develop a short, compact, and efficient barrel path by eliminating casting or looping in the swing.
description: Set the tee up with a net placed just behind the hitter’s back shoulder, forcing a tight, "hand-led" turn. If the hitter "casts" their hands or has a long "loop" in their swing, the bat will strike the net behind them. This drill is essential for developing a short, compact swing path to the ball.

name: Backhand Cross And Throw
goal: To develop an infielder's ability to cleanly field a backhand ground ball and execute a quick, powerful throw across the diamond.
description: An infielder starts in a ready position and moves to their backhand side to field a ground ball. After securing the ball, they use a quick crossover step with their throwing-side foot to open their hips and shoulders toward the target. The drill finishes with a strong, accurate throw to first base, emphasizing a fluid transition from fielding to throwing.

name: Backhand Pick & Stick Fungo
goal: To develop proper footwork, glove action, and body control while executing a clean backhand play on a ground ball.
description: An infielder sets up in a ready position as a coach hits a medium-paced fungo ground ball to their backhand side. The player must move their feet to get into a proper fielding position, extend their glove across their body to receive the ball, and secure it cleanly. Upon receiving the ball, the player should 'stick' the position for a moment, demonstrating balance and control through the backhand fielding motion.

name: Backhand Rake Fungo
goal: To develop an infielder's ability to attack backhand ground balls with proper footwork and a clean, continuous glove action.
description: An infielder begins in a ready stance and breaks to their backhand side to field a ground ball from a fungo bat. The player should focus on taking an aggressive angle to get around the ball, staying low while sweeping the glove across the ground in a "raking" motion to secure the ball. This drill emphasizes fluid footwork and maintaining momentum through the play to make a strong throw.

name: Backside Angle Toss
goal: To improve late tracking ability and reinforce a direct, efficient path to the baseball.
description: The tosser stands behind the hitter and flips the ball into the hitting zone from behind the shoulder. The hitter must track the ball as it enters their peripheral vision and react quickly, which improves "late" tracking and forces a direct path to the ball.

name: Balance Beam
goal: To enhance core stability and balance throughout the swing, ensuring a controlled and centered finish.
description: Perform tee work while standing on a 2x4 wooden beam or a specialized balance rail to challenge stability. If the hitter reaches too far or falls off balance during the follow-through, it indicates a flaw in their center of gravity. This builds a rock-solid foundation and core control.

name: Balance Drill
goal: To develop a stable and repeatable balance point, which is crucial for consistent mechanics and command.
description: From the stretch position, the pitcher will come set, lift their lead leg to a balanced position, and hold for a count of three to five seconds before returning to the set position. The focus is on maintaining a stable head and a controlled posture over the landing leg without wobbling. This drill should be repeated multiple times to build muscle memory and core stability.

name: Balance Pause Drill
goal: To develop body control and a consistent, powerful posture at the top of the pitching delivery.
description: Start on the pitching rubber with a slight weight bias on your drive leg. Lift your lead leg to your balance point, pausing for a full three to five seconds to ensure your posture is stable and your head is centered over your back leg. If you lose balance, reset and repeat the hold, focusing on a strong core and a controlled, deliberate motion before proceeding with your throw.

name: Balanced Stationary Drill
goal: To isolate the upper body, developing proper core engagement and extension in the pitching motion without lower body involvement.
description: Begin in a balanced, athletic stance with your glove-side shoulder aimed at your target, mimicking your landing position. Your throwing-side foot is anchored behind you as if on the pitching rubber. Execute the throw by focusing on upper body mechanics, emphasizing core engagement and full extension through the release, all while keeping your back foot firmly planted to maintain balance.

name: Balanced Stationary Drill With Upper Body
goal: To refine upper body mechanics and improve balance by isolating the throwing motion from the lower half.
description: Begin in a balanced, athletic stance with your feet shoulder-width apart, simulating the 'set' position. Without moving your lower body, isolate your upper half and execute your full pitching motion, focusing on arm path and shoulder rotation. Concentrate on maintaining a stable core and a consistent release point throughout the movement.

name: Ball Down Front Toss
goal: To teach the hitter proper body mechanics and barrel path required to effectively hit low pitches.
description: The coach tosses the ball specifically to the bottom of the strike zone, requiring the hitter to stay in their legs and maintain a proper spine angle. This prevents "reaching" with the hands and teaches the hitter how to elevate low pitches by getting the barrel underneath the ball correctly.

name: Ball Hit Front Toss
goal: To enhance pitch recognition, cognitive processing, and swing discipline by forcing the hitter to make immediate, accurate decisions on whether to swing or take the pitch.
description: The coach holds two balls and yells "Ball" or "Hit" just before the toss; if "Hit" is called, the hitter swings, but if "Ball" is called, the hitter must take the pitch. This sharpens cognitive processing and discipline, ensuring the hitter is tracking the ball all the way into the zone.

name: Ball In Hand
goal: To develop a quick, clean exchange and reinforce the proper footwork needed to make an accurate throw.
description: Begin in a standard infield athletic position with a baseball already secured in your glove. On a coach's signal, simulate the process of fielding a ground ball by transitioning the ball from your glove to your throwing hand as you shuffle your feet toward the target. This drill removes the variable of fielding a live ball, allowing the player to concentrate solely on a clean exchange and proper footwork alignment before the throw.

name: Ball In The Sun
goal: To train outfielders to confidently track and catch fly balls while effectively shielding their eyes from direct sunlight.
description: The drill starts with an outfielder positioned in their ready stance as a coach throws a high fly ball directly into the sun's path. The player must track the ball while using their glove to shield their eyes from the glare, timing the catch perfectly without losing sight of the ball. This exercise simulates the challenge of making a play when the sun is a major visual obstruction, forcing the player to rely on technique and feel.

name: Band Assisted Hip Hinge
goal: The primary goal of the Band-Assisted Hip Hinge Drill is to force the hitter to maintain a proper hip hinge throughout the swing

name: Bat Behind Hips
goal: To isolate the lower body and core mechanics to teach the proper sequence of hip-led rotation in the swing.
description: Hold the bat horizontally against the small of the back, hooked into the elbows, and practice dry rotations toward a tee. This drill isolates the lower body and core, showing the hitter how the hips should lead the swing without relying on the arms to create movement.

name: Bat Between Feet Drill
goal: Tio get hitters to maintain a strong, stable back foot connection to the ground while using a proper hip hinge. This leads to better rotation, more power, and a more controlled swing that finishes in

name: Beginner'S Batting Practice
goal: To establish foundational hitting confidence and muscle memory through consistent stance, full follow-through, and high-volume repetitions.
description: Focus on high-volume, easy-to-hit tosses directly over the heart of the plate to build confidence and muscle memory. The emphasis is on a consistent starting stance and a full, balanced follow-through. It is the foundational step for establishing a "feel" for the barrel.

name: Behind The Hitter
goal: Custom Drill

name: Behind the Hitter Toss 1-2-3
goal: To enhance elite reaction time and hand speed by forcing the hitter to execute the swing based on immediate visual recognition and rhythm.
description: Combine the 1-2-3 rhythm drill with a toss coming from behind the hitter's back hip. The hitter counts "one, two" as they load, and the coach tosses on "three," forcing the hitter to react to a ball they didn't see until the last moment. This builds elite reaction time and hand speed.

name: Belly Button Tee
goal: To reinforce a flat, powerful swing plane through the middle of the strike zone on pitches at belt height.
description: Place the tee at the height of the hitter's navel to practice hitting the "belt-high" strike. Hitters often struggle with this height by either "chopping" down or "looping" up; this drill reinforces a flat, powerful plane through the middle of the zone.

name: Bottom Hand Tee
goal: To strengthen the lead hand's pulling action and ensure the lead arm maintains a connected, direct path to the baseball.
description: Choke up on the bat and use only the lead hand to swing at the ball on the tee. This strengthens the "pulling" motion of the swing and ensures the lead arm doesn't "wing out" or become disconnected from the body. It is vital for maintaining a direct path to the ball.

name: Bow and Arrow Drill
goal: Teach hitters to feel and maintain proper separation by reinforcing scap load, rear-side stability, and the connection between the upper and lower half.

name: Box Drill 2nd Baseman Double Play Feeds
goal: To develop the footwork, rhythm, and quickness required for a second baseman to efficiently field a ground ball and make an accurate feed to turn a double play.
description: Set up four cones in a square to create a 'box' around the second base position. The player starts in the center of the box, and on a ground ball rolled from a coach, they will break to the ball, field it cleanly, and execute a quick, accurate feed to the shortstop covering the bag. This drill emphasizes efficient footwork and creating proper angles to the bag to ensure a smooth double play turn.

name: Box Drill 2nd Baseman Double Play Feeds
Box Drill Shortstop Double Play Feeds
goal: To improve a shortstop's ability to make quick, accurate, and efficient feeds to second base while maintaining proper body control in the turn of a double play.
description: Set up a square box of cones around the shortstop position to create a confined space for movement. On a ground ball from a coach, the shortstop fields the ball and executes a fundamentally sound feed to a partner at second base, focusing on a quick transfer and proper footwork within the box. The drill should incorporate feeds from different angles, including underhand tosses and backhand flips, to simulate various game situations.

name: Box Drill Shortstop Double Play Feeds
Break Down And Come Through
goal: To develop the fundamental footwork and body control required for an outfielder to properly field a ground ball and transition into a throw.
description: An outfielder approaches a ground ball, chopping their feet to get into a controlled 'break down' position with their glove-side foot forward. As they receive the ball, they 'come through' it, securing the catch out in front and transitioning their feet and body into a powerful and aligned throwing position. This drill emphasizes fielding the ball cleanly and preparing for a quick, accurate throw.

name: Break Of The Hands Drill
goal: To develop a smooth and repeatable hand break, ensuring a consistent arm path and proper timing in the pitching delivery.
description: Begin in a standard pitching stance, coming to a balanced position. As you initiate your motion, concentrate on a clean and efficient separation of your hands. The throwing hand should move down, back, and up in a fluid arc, while the glove hand provides balance and direction toward the target. This exercise isolates the critical 'hand break' to build muscle memory for a consistent arm action.

name: Chair Drill
goal: To develop lower-body strength and explosive power from the ground up, improving drive and balance in the pitching delivery.
description: Sit on the edge of a sturdy chair or stool with your feet in your pitching stance, simulating your position on the mound. Drive up from the chair as you begin your throwing motion, focusing on using your legs and glutes to generate power. Explode out of the seated position, ensuring your hips and shoulders rotate in sequence toward the target. This drill isolates the lower half, forcing the pitcher to engage their leg muscles for momentum rather than relying solely on their arm.

name: Change Up Catch
goal: Keep arm speed same as it would be for fastball, and keep good mechanics
description: The catcher sets up in their standard receiving position while the pitcher throws exclusively change-ups. The catcher must focus on tracking the pitch out of the hand, recognizing the reduced velocity, and staying balanced to receive the ball cleanly in the pocket of the mitt. This drill emphasizes patience and a soft glove hand to avoid stabbing at the slower pitch.

name: Change Up Grips
goal: To develop a repeatable change-up grip that maximizes deception and control.
description: This drill focuses on finding a comfortable and effective change-up grip. Experiment with various grips like the circle change, three-finger, or vulcan change, focusing on how each feels in your hand and upon release. The key is to maintain your natural arm speed and slot, allowing the grip itself to reduce the pitch's velocity and create deception.

name: Change-Up Front Toss
goal: To build "stay back" discipline, teaching the hitter to recognize speed changes and hold their weight back.
description: The coach mixes in slower, higher-arc tosses with standard front flips without warning. The hitter must recognize the speed difference and "wait" with their lower half, avoiding the common mistake of lunging forward. This builds "stay back" discipline and adjustability.

name: Change-Up Tee
goal: To train "mid-stride adjustability," forcing the hitter to change their timing and hand path based on a last-second cue.
description: Set up two tees at different heights and depths, and have a partner call out which one to hit just as the hitter starts their stride. This forces the hitter to adjust their mechanics mid-motion, simulating the timing change required to hit an off-speed pitch.

name: Color Front Toss
goal: To force "active" visual tracking, ensuring the hitter's eyes are locked onto the ball's details rather than just its general path.
description: Use balls with different colored dots or seams and have the hitter call out the color before making contact. This forces the eyes to focus intensely on the ball rather than just "zoning out," which significantly improves tracking and contact quality.

name: Crossover Drill
goal: The goal of the crossover hitting drill is to improve lower-body control, sequencing, and balance by forcing the hitter to feel proper weight transfer and hip rotation, helping eliminate spinning, lun

name: Crow Hops
goal: To maximize throwing velocity and accuracy from the outfield by creating linear momentum toward the target.
description: After fielding the ball, an outfielder takes a small, athletic hop forward, landing on their throwing-side foot while bringing their glove-side foot forward. This explosive step helps to align the body toward the target and generate momentum through the lower half. The player then transfers this energy into a powerful and accurate throw, ensuring the ball reaches its destination with velocity.

name: Daily Band Work
goal: To prepare the arm for pitching by doing a band workout to stretch and strengthen.
description: Secure an exercise band to a stable object at hip height, then perform a series of internal and external rotations with your throwing arm to stretch and strengthen the shoulder. The routine includes a variety of movements at different heights to ensure a comprehensive warm-up. This drill is designed to prepare the arm for the demands of pitching through a dynamic warm-up.

name: Daily Flat Ground Work
goal: To develop and reinforce proper pitching mechanics, arm care, and consistency in a low-stress environment.
description: From a flat ground position, the pitcher will throw to a catcher at a reduced distance, focusing on clean and repeatable mechanics. The pitcher should concentrate on maintaining a stable head position, a smooth and controlled arm action, and a balanced follow-through. This drill emphasizes precision and consistency over velocity, allowing the pitcher to build a solid mechanical foundation without the strain of throwing from a mound.

name: Daily Throwing Program
goal: To build a consistent, repeatable throwing motion and establish a solid mechanical foundation before progressing to more intensive pitching work.
description: Begin close to your partner, focusing on a clean and efficient arm path with a full follow-through. Use a four-seam grip and concentrate on spinning the ball with backspin. As you feel loose, gradually increase the distance, ensuring your mechanics remain consistent and your lower half is engaged to support the throw.

name: Decline Swings
goal: To prevent "drifting" by using gravity to force the hitter to keep their weight centered over their back leg during the stride.
description: Stand on a slight downward slope (or use a decline board) to practice staying back against gravity. This prevents the hitter from "rushing" down the mound and teaches them how to keep their weight centered over their back leg even when the body wants to drift forward.

name: Defense Prep
goal: To activate the player's defensive mindset and reinforce the fundamental mechanics of fielding ground balls.
description: Begin in a low, athletic fielding stance with your glove presented out front. Have a coach or partner roll ground balls directly to you, concentrating on watching the ball into the glove and securing it with both hands. This drill emphasizes clean mechanics and prepares you for reacting to various types of ground balls.

name: Defense Stance
goal: To improve strike zone awareness and "quiet" the body during the take, allowing the hitter to track the ball all the way in.
description: Practice "taking" pitches while in a full hitting stance, focusing on the ability to stay balanced and "track" the ball into the catcher's mitt without flinching. This builds "strike zone awareness" and helps hitters realize they don't have to swing at everything they see.

name: Double Ball Toss
goal: To sharpen rapid decision-making and hand-eye coordination by forcing the hitter to target one specific ball out of two.
description: The coach tosses two balls at once, one high and one low, and calls out "High" or "Low" after the balls are in the air. The hitter must quickly identify the correct target and execute a swing, which develops rapid decision-making and hand-eye coordination.

name: Double Play Footwork 2b Drop Step
goal: To create an efficient path to the bag and develop a quick, powerful throwing motion when turning a double play.
description: From the second base position, receive a feed from the shortstop or a coach. As the ball travels, execute a drop step with your throwing-side foot, opening your hips toward the base to create a direct line for your throw. Secure the catch as you move through the bag, then make a strong, accurate throw to first base to complete the double play.

name: Double Play Footwork 2b Rocker Step
goal: To develop a quick and efficient method for turning a double play at second base, ensuring a rapid transfer and powerful throw to first.
description: As the throw approaches, the second baseman should step on the corner of the base with their left foot while receiving the ball. Immediately after securing the catch, they rock back onto their right foot, creating a clear throwing lane and generating momentum. The final step is to stride with the left foot towards first base to execute a strong and accurate throw.

name: Double Play Footwork 2b Step Across
goal: To develop efficient footwork and a quick, powerful throw for turning double plays at second base.
description: The second baseman starts by approaching the bag as the throw comes from the shortstop or third baseman. As the ball arrives, the player should plant their left foot on the base, then step across the bag with their right foot toward the direction of the throw to create momentum and a clear throwing lane to first base. This drill emphasizes receiving the ball in the center of the body and executing a quick, fluid transfer for a strong, accurate throw.

name: Double Play Footwork Ss Back Cross
Double Play Footwork Ss Back Cross
goal: To train a shortstop's footwork for efficiently redirecting momentum towards first base, ensuring a strong and accurate throw to complete the double play.
description: The shortstop receives a feed at second base, and as they clear the bag to throw to first, their right foot crosses behind their left. This 'back cross' step helps to open the hips and align the body towards the target. The focus is on a quick, fluid transition from receiving the ball to generating powerful and accurate momentum on the throw.

name: Double Play Footwork Ss Front Cross
goal: To develop a shortstop's ability to execute a quick and efficient turn on a double play, particularly on balls hit to their glove-hand side.
description: From the shortstop position, field a ground ball moving to your glove side. Instead of a traditional drop step, your throwing side foot will cross over in front of your glove side foot as you secure the ball. This 'front cross' creates a direct line and powerful momentum for a quick, accurate throw to the second base bag to initiate the double play.

name: Double Play Footwork Ss Front Cross
Double Play Footwork Ss Pop Feet
Double Play Footwork Ss Pop Feet
goal: To train shortstops to quickly and explosively align their feet and body to make a strong, accurate throw to second base.
description: From the shortstop position, field a ground ball moving towards the second base bag. As you secure the ball, execute a quick 'pop' or hop with your feet, landing in a balanced position with your shoulders and hips aligned to your target. This explosive footwork allows for a rapid transfer and a powerful, accurate throw, minimizing the time it takes to turn the double play.

name: Double Tee
goal: To master swing plane consistency and "leveling out" the barrel at the exact moment of impact for line drives.
description: Set two tees up in a line, one in front of the other. The hitter must swing and hit the front ball without knocking over the back tee, or vice-versa. This reinforces a specific swing plane, teaching the hitter how to "level out" the barrel at the right moment.

name: Double Tee Decision Making
goal: To simulate split-second adjustments for inner vs.
description: Place two tees at different locations (inner and outer). As the hitter begins their load, the coach shouts "Inside" or "Outside," and the hitter must adjust their hands and path to hit the corresponding ball. This simulates the split-second adjustments needed in a real at-bat.

name: Drag Bunt
goal: To disguise the bunt until the last moment and beat out the throw by placing the ball in a difficult spot for the defense to field.
description: As the pitcher starts their delivery, the batter takes a crossover step with their back foot, simultaneously opening their hips and shoulders toward the field. The bat is brought to a level position, with the top hand guiding the barrel to angle the ball down the desired baseline. The objective is to make contact with the ball out in front of the plate while already in motion, allowing the batter to get a quick start out of the box.

name: Drop Step Cones
goal: To develop a quick and efficient first step, enabling the outfielder to react and get in position to catch deep fly balls. (Edited inline)
description: Set up three cones in a triangle, with the player starting at the apex. On the coach's signal, the player executes a drop step, opening their hips and shoulders toward one of the back cones, then sprints to that cone. This drill emphasizes creating a direct line to the ball and gaining ground efficiently on balls hit over the outfielder's head.

name: Drop Step Groundballs
goal: To develop an infielder's ability to create space and efficient angles to the ball on groundballs hit directly at them or to their immediate left or right.
description: From an athletic fielding position, the player initiates the drill by taking a quick drop step with the foot corresponding to the direction of the groundball. This action involves opening the hips and stepping back to create a better angle to approach the ball. After the drop step, the player aggressively attacks the ball, fields it cleanly, and simulates a throw to the appropriate base.

name: Elevated Front Toss Drill
goal: The goal of the Elevated Tee Drill is to help hitters adjust to high pitch locations by improving swing plane, barrel accuracy, and posture, teaching them to stay on top of the ball without dropping t

name: Execute On The Field
goal: To develop a player's ability to execute fundamental defensive plays flawlessly under pressure.
description: This drill simulates live game situations, requiring infielders to field ground balls hit by a coach and make accurate throws to the correct base. Players should focus on anticipating the play, maintaining proper defensive posture, and executing a clean exchange from glove to hand. The objective is to complete each defensive opportunity successfully, reinforcing the importance of making routine plays consistently.

name: Extended Tee
goal: To encourage "full extension" through the ball, ensuring the hitter drives through contact rather than cutting the swing short.
description: Place the tee significantly further out in front of the plate than usual to practice "extension." The hitter focuses on hitting through the ball and reaching a full "V" shape with their arms after contact, ensuring they aren't "cutting off" their swing too early.

name: Extension and Direction Drill
goal: Help hitters develop a proper bat path that produces a more upward swing plane to get the ball in the air, reducing excessive topspin and weak grounders.

name: Fastball Front Toss
goal: To eliminate "extra" movements in the load, forcing the hitter to be efficient to catch up to higher velocity.
description: The coach throws firmer, flatter tosses from a shorter distance to simulate high-velocity pitching. This reduces the hitter's reaction time and forces them to eliminate any "extra" movements in their load, promoting a "short to the ball, long through the ball" philosophy.

name: Flamingo (One Leg Balanced) Drill
goal: Develop stride control and proper weight distribution by teaching the hitter to stay loaded on the back hip, ride the back leg forward, and create separation without drifting or lunging.

name: Flat Ground
goal: Goal: Focus on having a good extension and a smooth follow-through.
description: From a set position on a flat surface, the pitcher simulates a full throwing motion, focusing on a clean and repeatable arm action without the strain of a mound. The pitcher should concentrate on maintaining balance, achieving good extension toward the target, and finishing with a smooth, controlled follow-through. This drill emphasizes consistent mechanics over velocity, making it ideal for warm-ups and reinforcing proper form.

name: Flaw - Casting the Hands Outside of the Ball
Flaw - Contact Point Too Far Out Front
Flaw: Casting The Hands Outside Of The Ball
goal: To correct a "long" swing by forcing the hands to stay "inside" a barrier on the inner half of the plate.
description: To fix this, place a tee or a soft barrier on the outside of the plate and practice hitting balls on the inside corner. The hitter must keep their hands "tucked" and lead with the knob to avoid hitting the barrier, effectively curing the "long" swing path.

name: Flaw: Chopping At The Ball
goal: To correct a steep downward attack angle, forcing the hitter to level out and drive through the back of the ball.
description: Place a tee at the top of the strike zone and focus on hitting "line drives" rather than ground balls. The hitter must feel the barrel stay "on plane" with the ball rather than attacking it at a steep downward angle, which often results in weak contact.

name: Flaw: Collapsing The Backside
goal: To maintain a vertical spine and high back shoulder, preventing the "dip" that causes pop-ups.
description: Place a tall tee or a PVC pipe behind the hitter’s back side to keep the spine vertical. The hitter practices rotating "around" their axis rather than "dipping" their back shoulder, which helps maintain a level swing and prevents popping up.

name: Flaw: Contact Point Too Far Out Front
goal: To train the hitter to "let the ball travel," proving they can generate power even when hitting the ball deep in the zone.
description: Move the tee back toward the hitter's back foot and practice driving the ball to the opposite field. This teaches the hitter to "let the ball travel" and realize they can still generate power even when the ball is deep in the zone.

name: Flaw: Hands Dropping Too Low
goal: To encourage a "direct" hand path from the launch position, eliminating the "hitch" that wastes time.
description: Place a "high tee" in the zone and require the hitter to hit line drives. If the hands drop, the hitter will swing "under" the ball or hit the tee itself. This encourages a more direct, downward-to-level hand path from the starting position.

name: Flaw: Improper Stance
goal: To create a consistent, athletic "starting box" through repeated visual and physical checks of the setup.
description: Use a mirror or video analysis to check that the feet are shoulder-width apart, knees are athletic, and hands are in a "loaded" position. Repeatedly stepping into the box and "checking" these markers builds the muscle memory needed for a consistent starting point.

name: Flaw: Lack Of Separation
goal: To create "torque" by ensuring the hands stay back as the front foot moves forward during the stride.
description: Use a resistance band anchored behind the hitter and held in the lead hand. As the hitter strides forward, they must feel the band "stretch" as the hands stay back. This creates the "rubber band effect" necessary for elite bat speed and power.

name: Flaw: Loop In The Barrel
goal: To eliminate "barrel drag" by using a wall constraint that forces the hitter to stay "knob-first" into the zone.
description: Perform the "Wall Drill" where the hitter stands with their back a bat-length away from a wall. During the swing, if the barrel "loops" or "drags," it will strike the wall. This forces a "knob-first" entry into the hitting zone.

name: Flaw: Lunging To The Ball
goal: To keep the center of gravity stable, ensuring the head doesn't move forward during the swing.
description: Practice hitting off a tee while keeping the front heel off the ground until the last possible second. This forces the weight to stay on the "inside" of the back leg, preventing the common "lunge" where the head moves too far forward before the swing.

name: Flaw: No Lower Half
goal: To "wake up" the hips and glutes through exaggerated movement, teaching the hitter that the legs drive the barrel.
description: Practice "Happy Gilmore" style swings where the hitter steps behind their lead foot and then forward into the swing. This exaggerated movement forces the hips to engage and creates a feeling of "driving" through the ball using the large muscles of the legs.

name: Flaw: Soft Front Knee
goal: To create a "firm front side," providing a solid post for the body to rotate around to prevent power leakage.
description: Place a firm object or a "slant board" under the front foot to encourage a "firm front side" upon impact. A "soft" or bent front knee leaks power; this drill helps the hitter "post up" and transfer all their energy into the barrel.

name: Flaw: Stepping In The Bucket
goal: To fix a "leaking" stride path by using a barrier that forces the front foot to stay directed toward the pitcher.
description: Place a bat or a 2x4 on the ground behind the hitter's lead foot. If the hitter "steps in the bucket" (steps toward third base for a righty), they will trip or step on the object. This reinforces a straight, "toward the pitcher" stride path.

name: Fly Balls
goal: To develop the fundamental skills of tracking, judging, and catching fly balls in the outfield.
description: From a ready athletic position, the outfielder reads the trajectory of a fly ball hit by a coach or partner. The player should take a direct route to the ball, getting their body in position to make the catch. Emphasis is placed on using proper footwork to get under the ball and securing it with two hands.

name: Follow Through Swing
goal: To ensure total body balance and completion of the swing, finishing with the chest toward the pitcher.
description: Focus entirely on the finish of the swing, ensuring the bat finishes high and the chest is facing the pitcher. The hitter should be able to "hold" their finish for three seconds without wobbling, indicating perfect balance throughout the entire motion.

name: Forehand Range
goal: To increase an infielder's lateral range and ability to make a strong throw after fielding a ball on the move.
description: From their ready position, the infielder reacts to a ground ball hit moderately to their forehand side, focusing on taking an efficient route to the ball. The player should work on fielding the ball outside of their body while keeping their feet moving toward the target to ensure a quick and powerful throw. This drill emphasizes creating proper angles to the ball and maintaining momentum through the play.

name: Four Corners
goal: To develop advanced fielding skills, including quick footwork, soft hands, and the ability to make accurate throws under pressure from all infield positions.
description: Set up at the four corners of the infield, with players at first, second, third, and shortstop. Coaches hit a variety of hard ground balls to each player, who must field the ball cleanly and make a strong, accurate throw to the next base. The drill should include reps for routine plays, backhands, and forehands, keeping a quick pace to simulate game-like pressure.

name: Free Play
goal: To develop a player's natural defensive instincts, reaction time, and fundamental fielding and throwing mechanics in a game-like environment.
description: A coach hits or throws a variety of ground balls to infielders, who are free to move to any position. Players should focus on anticipating the ball's path, getting into a solid fielding stance, and securing the ball cleanly in their glove. After fielding, the player should make a crisp, accurate throw to a designated base, practicing fluid footwork and a quick release.

name: Front Hip Toss
goal: To train the hitter to "clear the hips" quickly and stay inside the ball when facing tight inside pitches.
description: The tosser stands in front and slightly to the side, flipping balls toward the hitter's front hip. This requires the hitter to "clear" their hips quickly and stay "inside" a ball that is "crowding" them, which is essential for hitting inside fastballs.

name: Fungo Charge
goal: To develop the technique and confidence needed to attack slowly hit balls, make plays on the run, and execute a quick transfer for a successful putout.
description: From your ready position, aggressively charge the slow-rolling ground ball hit by the fungo bat. Focus on timing your approach to field the ball on a short hop or out in front of your body, often with your bare hand. Secure the grip quickly and make a strong, accurate throw to first base while still moving towards your target.

name: Fungo for Hitters
goal: To develop total "body-to-barrel" coordination by having the hitter time their own toss and swing fluidly.
description: Have the hitter throw the ball up to themselves and hit it into the field, just like a coach. This build "hand-eye-barrel" coordination and teaches the hitter how to "time up" their own body movements to make solid contact.

name: Gain Ground Drill
goal: To teach hitters to properly unanchor their back foot during hip rotation, promoting effective weight transfer and preventing getting stuck on the backside.

name: Get on Plane Drill
goal: The goal is to train hitters to keep the bat on a flatter, more level path by focusing on hip rotation and staying behind the ball during the swing. main focus: The main focus is on turning the hips p

name: Groundball On Knees Progression
goal: To develop soft hands, proper glove position, and the skill of fielding the ball on the hop by isolating the upper body.
description: Players start on their knees, positioned 10 feet apart, or a coach can hit groundballs from 45 feet away. The focus is on keeping the glove out in front, watching the ball all the way in, and using soft hands to field the ball on a hop. This progression helps isolate the upper body and hands, forcing the player to work their glove underneath the ball.

name: Grounders
goal: To develop a fundamentally sound and aggressive approach to fielding ground balls in the outfield.
description: From a ready position, the outfielder charges a ground ball hit directly at them. The player should focus on approaching the ball under control, breaking down with their glove-side foot forward, and keeping their eyes on the ball as they field it out in front of their body. After securing the ball in their glove, they should cover it with their throwing hand to prepare for a quick transition.

name: Half Swing Drill
goal: To diagnose mechanical checkpoints at the "point of contact," ensuring optimal body position.
description: The hitter takes a swing but stops the barrel exactly at the "point of contact" and holds it. This allows the coach and player to check the "checkpoints": is the back knee bent? Are the eyes on the ball? Is the "V" in the hands correct? It’s a great diagnostic tool.

name: Hand Break Drill
goal: To develop a consistent and efficient hand separation, synchronizing the arm action with the lower body's movement to improve pitching mechanics and velocity.
description: From the stretch position, the pitcher comes to a set position and then lifts their leg. As the lead leg begins its descent, the hands separate from the glove in a downward and outward motion, keeping the fingers on top of the ball. This drill emphasizes a smooth and properly timed hand break, ensuring the throwing arm is in the correct position as the body moves toward the plate.

name: Happy Gilmore Drill
goal: To develop a hitter’s understanding and control of the back foot kickback movement, focusing on proper rhythm, timing, and deceleration during the swing.

name: Heavy Bat Drill
goal: Improve swing sequencing and bat speed by forcing the hitter to use their body instead of swinging with the arms.

name: Heavy Front Side Drill
goal: Work on balance and weight transfer in the direction of the target
description: Begin in your pitching stance with a slightly heavier ball, such as a weighted baseball or softball, in your glove hand to create a 'heavy' front side sensation. As you move through your delivery, focus on keeping your front shoulder and glove arm directed towards the target for as long as possible before rotation. The added weight provides a physical cue to maintain a strong, stable front side, preventing your upper body from flying open too early.

name: High Choppers
goal: To develop the skill of charging and fielding high-bouncing ground balls, improving the player's timing, footwork, and ability to make a quick transition from fielding to throwing.
description: From a ready position, the infielder charges a high-bouncing ground ball hit by a coach. The player must aggressively attack the ball, fielding it at the peak of its bounce before it starts to come down. After securing the ball, the player makes a strong and accurate throw to the target base.

name: High Choppers Fungo
goal: To develop an infielder's ability to aggressively charge and properly time the fielding of high-bouncing ground balls.
description: A coach hits high-bouncing ground balls, or 'choppers,' with a fungo bat toward an infielder at their regular position. The fielder's task is to read the trajectory of the bounce, charge the ball with controlled aggression, and time their approach to field it at the highest point of the hop. After securing the ball, the player should focus on making a strong, accurate throw to the designated base.

name: High Tee
goal: To train a direct hand path to the top of the strike zone, preventing the "loop" that misses high fastballs.
description: Set the tee at the very top of the strike zone (chest height). The hitter must stay "on top" of the ball and drive line drives. This is the ultimate "anti-loop" drill and forces the hitter to use their hands effectively to get the barrel to a high pitch.

name: Hip Loading Balance Disc Tee
goal: To build "core-to-ground" stability, forcing the athlete to engage large muscles to remain balanced while rotating.
description: The hitter stands with their back foot on an inflatable balance disc while hitting off a tee. The instability forces the hitter to engage their core and glutes to stay balanced, reinforcing a powerful and stable "backside" load.

name: Hip Velocity Drill
goal: Increase hip velocity and lower body involvement

name: Hips Fire First Tee Drill
goal: Hip-separation hitting drill that teaches athletes to fire the hips before the hands

name: Hit and Run
goal: To prioritize contact and direction over power, training the hitter to move a runner regardless of pitch location.
description: The coach tosses a ball and the hitter must put it on the ground, preferably toward the right side of the field. This simulates the "hit and run" play where contact and direction are more important than power, teaching bat control and situational awareness.

name: Hook 'Ems
goal: To develop proper hip sequencing and direction into foot-strike, promoting a powerful and efficient throwing motion.
description: From the peak of your leg lift, focus on setting the angle of your hips by driving your back glute toward the target. This initial move should be the catalyst for your entire throwing motion, ensuring your lower half leads the way. By properly sequencing this movement, you will create a more powerful and efficient energy transfer from the ground up, leading to increased velocity and better command.

name: Indoor Facility Defensive Stations
goal: To develop a range of fundamental infield skills in a structured and efficient station-based circuit.
description: Set up several stations in an indoor space, each targeting a specific infield skill. For instance, one station could focus on fielding groundballs rolled by a coach, another on receiving throws at a base, and a third on quick footwork through an agility ladder. Players rotate through each station, allowing for focused repetition on a variety of defensive fundamentals in a controlled setting.

name: Infield Hands Routine
goal: To develop an infielder's hand-eye coordination, quick transfers, and soft hands when fielding ground balls.
description: In this drill, an infielder assumes their ready position without a glove while a partner tosses them ground balls from a short distance. The player focuses on receiving the ball with two hands out in front, absorbing the impact with soft hands, and quickly transitioning the ball to their throwing hand. This routine helps build muscle memory for the proper fielding sequence.

name: Inside/Outside Tee
goal: To develop directional hitting versatility, teaching the distinct "feels" of pulling a ball vs.
description: Set up two tees—one on the inner half and one on the outer half. The hitter alternates swings, focusing on "pulling" the inside ball and "driving" the outside ball to the opposite gap. This teaches the different "contact points" required for different pitch locations.

name: Intentional Throwing
goal: To enhance a pitcher's body awareness and ability to make targeted mechanical adjustments.
description: This drill emphasizes deliberate practice over velocity. The pitcher focuses on a single mechanical point for each throw, such as hand position or follow-through, while throwing at a reduced intensity. Each repetition is a conscious effort to execute that specific movement perfectly, building a stronger mind-body connection.

name: Interval Throwing
goal: To safely build arm strength, improve endurance, and reinforce sound throwing mechanics through a structured progression.
description: Begin by making 15-20 easy throws from a distance of 45-60 feet, focusing on a clean arm action and consistent release point. After a short rest, increase the distance by 15-20 feet and repeat the set, ensuring you maintain the same smooth mechanics without overthrowing. This progression helps pitchers build arm strength and endurance gradually while reinforcing proper throwing fundamentals.

name: Jump Back Drill
goal: This drill helps hitters improve their hip rotation control and balance at contact, resulting in a more powerful and effective swing.

name: KaBoom Drill
goal: Build slow-to-fast rhythm — controlled load/stride (“Kaa”) followed by an explosive launch (“Boom”). Helps hitters stop rushing/drifting and sync foot plant with swing start.

name: Knee Short Hops
goal: To refine an infielder's glove skills and hand-eye coordination for balls that take difficult or unexpected hops.
description: From a position on both knees, an infielder sets up with their glove out in front, ready to receive. A coach or partner will toss firm short hops, challenging the player to keep their eyes on the ball and hands soft through the catch. The focus is on clean fielding mechanics and a quick transfer from glove to throwing hand without standing up.

name: Knee to Knee Tee
goal: To ensure a "full gather" on the back side, using an exaggerated leg lift to ensure weight is loaded.
description: During the load, the hitter brings their lead knee back until it almost touches their back knee before striding forward. This "exaggerated load" ensures the weight is fully gathered on the back side and helps hitters who have a "short" or "non-existent" stride.

name: Kneel to Hip Hinge Drill
goal: To develop a proper hip hinge during the load phase and promote controlled forward movement in the hitter’s swing.

name: Lincecum Drill
goal: To develop a dynamic, full-body pitching delivery that improves momentum and maximizes arm speed through proper kinetic sequencing.
description: From the windup, the pitcher should focus on creating a continuous and explosive motion toward the plate, avoiding any pauses. The drill emphasizes a strong drive from the back leg and leading the movement with the front hip to generate momentum. The throwing arm should remain relaxed and follow the lower body's rotation, ensuring it comes through late to create a powerful, whip-like action.

name: Location Tee
goal: To improve spatial awareness and adjustment, forcing the hitter to calibrate their path to different zones.
description: Move the tee to various spots in and around the strike zone (low and away, high and tight, etc.) without the hitter looking. The hitter must then step in, identify the location, and execute the correct swing for that specific spot.

name: Long Tee
goal: To maximize extension and intent, challenging the hitter to use their entire body to drive the ball for distance.
description: Place the tee on the home plate and aim for a target deep in the outfield (like the centerfield fence). This encourages the hitter to "stay through" the ball and use their full "extension" to maximize distance, rather than just "slapping" at the ball.

name: Long Toss
goal: To build foundational arm strength, improve throwing mechanics, and promote a loose, fluid arm action over extended distances.
description: Begin by playing catch at a comfortable distance, focusing on a loose and relaxed arm motion. Gradually extend the distance between you and your partner, throwing the ball with a high arc to ensure you are not throwing on a line. As you reach your maximum comfortable distance, work on maintaining a fluid, full range of motion on each throw before gradually decreasing the distance to cool down.

name: Low Tee
goal: To build leg strength and posture maintenance, teaching the hitter to get down to the ball with their legs, not their waist.
description: Set the tee as low as it can go, near the knees. The hitter must "sink" into their legs and maintain their "spine angle" to reach the ball without "bending at the waist." This builds leg strength and the ability to drive low pitches.

name: Low Tee Mini Bat
goal: To combine leg-driven posture with high-precision hand control using a smaller barrel on difficult low pitches.
description: Use a shortened "one-hand" bat to hit balls off a low tee. The smaller bat requires much more precision and forces the hitter to use their "small muscles" in the wrists and hands to control the barrel, while the low height keeps the legs engaged.

name: Mirror Drill
goal: The goal of the mirror drill is to develop proper hitting mechanics by providing immediate visual feedback, allowing players to self-correct and internalize key movements.

name: Net Drill
goal: The goal of the drill is to teach the hitter to properly load into the back hip, execute a controlled, balanced stride, and achieve a strong launch position. It also emphasizes creating separation bet

name: No Stride Tee
goal: To isolate rotation and hand path, removing the stride to focus on a clean, powerful turn.
description: The hitter starts with their feet already in the "wide" stride position and hits off the tee using only the rotation of their hips and hands. This isolates the "turn" and is excellent for hitters who have "too much head movement" during their stride.

name: Off-Speed Front Toss
goal: To train visual recognition of pitching cues, teaching the hitter to stay back when they see "slow" arm action.
description: The coach uses a "long" arm circle and a slow release to simulate a curveball or change-up. The hitter must recognize the "slower" motion and hold their "hinge" longer, teaching them how to not "over-commit" to the fastball.

name: On The Run
goal: To train infielders to make strong, accurate throws on plays where they do not have time to set their feet.
description: This drill simulates fielding a slow roller or bunt that requires the infielder to charge the ball and make a throw without stopping. The player should focus on creating a direct line to the ball, fielding it out in front, and then seamlessly transitioning into a throwing motion while their feet are still moving. The key is to synchronize the footwork with the throw, using the momentum of the body to generate power and accuracy on the run.

name: On The Run Fungo
goal: To develop an infielder's ability to read and react to difficult, game-like ground balls with topspin.
description: A coach or player tosses the ball to a batter who hits it on the run, creating a more game-like ground ball with topspin for the infielder. This drill forces the fielder to read the unpredictable hops and react quickly with their footwork, improving their defensive instincts. The difficulty can be increased by timing the play to simulate a runner heading to first base.

name: One Handed Hitting
goal: To isolate and identify weak links in the hand path, ensuring both hands are doing their specific jobs correctly.
description: Alternate between "Top Hand Only" and "Bottom Hand Only" swings using a short bat. This isolates the specific roles of each hand (bottom hand pulls, top hand directs/powers) and identifies which hand might be "weaker" or "dominant" in the swing.

name: One Handed Load Drill
goal: To develop a cleaner, more efficient load by simplifying the hitter’s motion and promoting proper hand and bat positioning through the one-handed load drill.

name: One Knee Drill
goal: To develop a consistent arm action, proper upper-body rotation, and a repeatable release point without interference from the lower half.
description: Begin in a balanced position with your throwing-side knee on the ground and your glove-side foot planted firmly in front. Without using your lower body, focus on a clean, fluid arm path, rotating your torso and shoulders to deliver the ball towards the target. This drill isolates the upper half, forcing you to concentrate on proper arm mechanics and a consistent release point out in front of your body.

name: One handed drills
goal: The goal of one-handed hitting drills is to isolate and strengthen the roles of the top and bottom hands in the swing so hitters improve barrel control, bat path, and power while developing better fee

name: Outfield Groundballs
goal: To develop the fundamental techniques for aggressively charging, properly fielding, and quickly transitioning into a throw for ground balls hit to the outfield.
description: From a ready position, the outfielder charges a ground ball hit by a coach or partner, focusing on taking an aggressive but controlled angle. The player should break down with feet apart to get their body in front of the ball and field it cleanly out in front. After securing the ball, the player executes a proper crow-hop to simulate a strong and accurate throw back to the infield.

name: Outside Angle Toss
goal: To master the away pitch, forcing the hitter to stay on the ball and drive it to the opposite gap.
description: The coach tosses the ball from a side angle so it "fades" away from the hitter. This forces the hitter to "reach" with their hands and "stay on" the ball, teaching them how to drive the "away" pitch into the opposite-field gap.

name: Outside Tee
goal: To reinforce the "let it travel" philosophy, ensuring the hitter hits the outside pitch deep in the zone.
description: Set the tee on the "outer black" of the plate and slightly "deeper" in the zone. The hitter focuses on hitting the "inside half" of the ball and driving it to the opposite field, reinforcing the "let it travel" mindset.

name: Overload Bat Soft Toss
goal: To build swing-specific strength, using resistance to force the body into efficient mechanics.
description: Use a "heavy bat" or a weighted "donut" during soft toss to build "functional strength" in the forearms and core. The extra weight forces the hitter to use "perfect mechanics" to move the bat, as "muscling it" with just the arms becomes too difficult.

name: PVC Stride Separation Drill
goal: To develop proper stepping mechanics and scapular activation during the load and stride phase of the baseball swing.

name: Power Position
goal: To develop a strong and repeatable body position that maximizes leverage and explosive force in the pitching delivery.
description: From a balanced starting position, the pitcher lifts their lead leg and drives toward the plate, freezing at the moment their front foot lands. In this power position, the throwing arm should be up and back in a loaded 'L' shape, with hips open and eyes on the target. Hold this pose for a few seconds to build muscle memory before following through without a ball.

name: Preset Backhand Pick & Stick
goal: To develop soft hands and a stable base by teaching the infielder to 'stick' the backhand catch without stabbing at the ball.
description: Begin in a preset backhand fielding position, with your glove low and open to the ground. As the ball is rolled in, receive it cleanly in the pocket of your glove and 'stick' the catch, freezing your position upon receiving the ball. This action emphasizes keeping the glove hand steady and absorbing the ball's force without stabbing at it, ensuring a secure catch.

name: Preset Backhand Rake
goal: To develop clean glove action and muscle memory for securing ground balls on the backhand side.
description: Start in a pre-set backhand fielding stance, with your glove already positioned open and low to the ground on your backhand side. As the ball is rolled, focus on attacking it with your glove, using a firm 'raking' motion from outside-in to secure the ball into the pocket. This drill isolates the final action of the backhand play, allowing for repetition and focus on proper glove mechanics without the need for complex footwork.

name: Push Bunt
goal: To develop the skill of bunting for a base hit by using a firm, controlled push to place the ball offensively.
description: From your batting stance, pivot to square your shoulders to the pitcher as the ball is delivered, bringing the bat out front. Instead of absorbing the pitch for a soft sacrifice, firmly guide the bat forward through the ball upon impact. The objective is to push the ball with pace past the pitcher and charging infielders, aiming for an open area on the infield for a base hit.

name: Quarterback Adjustments
goal: To improve an outfielder's ability to read, react, and adjust to fly balls hit in multiple directions.
description: The outfielder starts in a ready position while the coach stands in front of them with two balls. The coach throws one ball over the outfielder's left shoulder and the other over their right, forcing them to drop-step and adjust their route to catch the ball. This drill teaches outfielders to read the ball's trajectory and adjust their path accordingly, emphasizing efficient footwork and glove presentation.

name: Quarterback Angle Throw
goal: To train outfielders to make powerful and precise throws from various angles while on the run.
description: An outfielder starts by backpedaling away from the infield, then breaks at a 45-degree angle to simulate fielding a ball on the run. As they catch the ball, they will open their hips and make a strong, accurate throw to a designated base, mimicking the motion of a quarterback throwing on the move. This drill emphasizes a quick transition from fielding to throwing while maintaining momentum and accuracy.

name: Quarterback Over The Shoulder
goal: To develop an outfielder's ability to properly execute a drop step, efficiently track a deep fly ball, and make a difficult over-the-shoulder catch.
description: The outfielder starts in a ready position while a coach or partner stands behind them, acting as the 'quarterback.' The quarterback throws a ball high and deep over the outfielder's head, forcing them to execute a drop step, turn their hips, and run to track the ball. The player must locate the ball in the air and make a clean over-the-shoulder catch, simulating a game situation where a fly ball is hit over their head.

name: Quick Hands Fielding
goal: To improve an infielder's hand speed, reaction time, and ability to receive the ball with soft, absorbent hands.
description: From a low, athletic fielding position, a player prepares to receive a series of rapid tosses from a coach or partner standing a few feet away. The player focuses on using quick, soft hands to catch each ball cleanly out in front of their body, funnelling it to their center. This drill emphasizes minimal body movement, forcing the player to rely on efficient hand-eye coordination and swift glove-to-hand transfer.

name: Rainbow Route
goal: To teach outfielders the proper footwork and running path for efficiently tracking and catching deep fly balls hit over their head.
description: An outfielder starts in a ready position as a coach throws a deep fly ball over their head, forcing them to retreat. The player will take a drop step with the foot on the side the ball is thrown and run a curved "rainbow" route to get behind the ball. The focus is on maintaining eye contact with the ball while running the arc, allowing the player to catch the ball while moving forward toward their target.

name: Random Front Toss
goal: To simulate game-like unpredictability, forcing the hitter to rely on reaction and rhythm rather than guessing.
description: The coach varies the speed, location, and "timing" of the tosses with no predictable pattern. This is the most "game-like" form of front toss and forces the hitter to "react" rather than "anticipate," which is the core of successful hitting.

name: Rapid Fire Fungo
goal: To improve an infielder's reaction time, footwork, and ability to handle multiple plays in quick succession under pressure.
description: A coach hits a rapid succession of ground balls to an infielder, who must field each one cleanly and make a quick throw to a designated base. The player should maintain a ready athletic stance, focusing on efficient footwork to move to each ball and a quick, clean exchange to get the ball out of the glove. This drill simulates the fast pace of a real game, forcing the infielder to react and reset quickly between plays.

name: Rapid Fire Toss
goal: To improve fast-twitch recovery and minimalist mechanics, forcing the hitter to be quick to keep up.
description: The coach flips balls one after another as fast as the hitter can reset. This "burnout" drill builds "fast-twitch" muscle fibers and forces the hitter to find the most "efficient" path to the ball to keep up with the pace.

name: Read And React
goal: To develop an outfielder's ability to get a quick and accurate jump on the ball by improving their reaction time and first-step efficiency.
description: From a ready athletic position, the outfielder's focus is entirely on the coach or hitter. As the ball is hit or thrown, the player reads the angle and speed off the bat to take a quick, explosive first step in the correct direction—forward, back, left, or right. The drill is completed after the initial movement, emphasizing the 'read and react' phase over catching the ball.

name: Reverse Flamingo Drill
goal: To develop proper sequencing in the baseball swing by promoting a strong, stable front leg and effective rotational movement through controlled balance.

name: Reverse Stride
goal: Improve directional hitting, extension through the ball, and the correct palm up / palm down hand position at contact — training the hitter to stay on plane and drive the ball where it's intended to g
description: The hitter starts with their "front foot" back and "steps forward" into the box as the ball is tossed. This creates "forward momentum" and helps hitters who are "stagnant" or "too quiet" in their load to feel what "athletic movement" feels like.

name: Rhythm Drill 1-2 Step
